coredata.dc:title: Dual-photocathode array propelled lab-on-paper ratiometric photoelectrochemical sensing platform for ultrasensitive microRNA bioassay
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hongmei
Xu, Meiling
Li, Zhenglin
Ge, Shenguang
Zhang, Lina
Zhu, Peihua
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a dual-photocathode array propelled lab-on-paper ratiometric photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform integrated with DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy was engineered for ultrasensitive assay of microRNA-141 (miRNA-141). Specifically, the spatial-resolved dual-photocathode array were comprised of a working electrode (PCE1) and internal reference electrode (PCE2), which were responsible for producing independent working signal (Iw ) and internal reference signal (Ir ). The dual-photocathode array was assembled with cascading multiple photosensitive structures consisting of pyramid-shaped Cu2O, graphene quantum dots, and AgI nanoparticles (NPs). With the proximity regulation of DNA hairpins H1 and H2, the amplified photocurrent signals were obtained on PCE1 and PCE2. When target existed, the output DNA probes from various concentrations of miRNA-141 induced duplex specific nuclease-catalyzed target recycling reactions were introduced onto PCE1, while the output DNA probes produced with a constant concentration of miRNA-141 were introduced onto PCE2, resulting in the formation of different amounts of DNA bridge nanostructures on PCE1 and PCE2. Those formed DNA bridge nanostructures with enhanced rigidity remarkably increased the electron-transfer tunneling distance between the sensing interface and AgI NPs immobilized on the terminals of H1 and H2, leading to noticeably decreased photocurrent signals. Based on the introduction of different amounts of target-induced output DNA probes on PCE1 and PCE2, the variable Iw and constant Ir were ultimately collected. By monitoring the ratio of Iw and Ir , the ultrasensitive bioassay of miRNA-141 was realized with high accuracy, selectivity, and practicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-photocathode array
Ratiometric
Photoelectrochemical
DNA bridge nanostructure
Pyramid-shaped Cu2O

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and operation of ratiometric PEC paper analytical device
Electrodeposition of pyramid-shaped Cu2O on dual-photocathode array
Construction of ratiometric PEC sensing array
Analytical performance of paper-based ratiometric PEC sensing platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A-C) Cu2O/Au-PCE, (D and E) Au-PCE. (F) EDS spectrum and EDS element mapping images (inset) of Cu2O/Au-PCE.
XPS spectra of Cu2O/Au-PCE: (A) Survey, (B) Cu 2p, and (C) O 1s, (D) Au 4f. (E) XRD patterns of (a) bare paper, (b) Au-PCE, and (c) Cu2O/Au-PCE. (F) Raman spectrum of Cu2O/Au-PCE.
(A) TEM image, (B) Size distribution, and (C) EDS spectrum of AgI NPs. XPS spectra of AgI NPs: (D) Ag 3d and (E) I 3d. (F) XRD pattern of AgI NPs.

(A) EIS of PCE1 under different modification conditions. (B) Photocurrent responses of the modified Au-PCE at different stages: (a) after Cu2O electrodeposition, (b) after GQDs sensitization, (c) after H1-AgI and H2-AgI immobilization, (d) after MCH blocking, and (e) after DNA bridge nanostructures assembly.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the proposed biosensor toward various concentrations of miRNA-141 on PCE1 (from a to i: 0.2, 2.0, 10, 100, 103, 104, 105, 5 × 105, 106 fM) and 1 pM miRNA-141 on PCE2. (B) The linear relationship of Iw /Ir versus the logarithm of miRNA-141 concentrations. (C) Selectivity of the PEC biosensor treated with (a) blank, (b) miRNA-21, (c) miRNA-155, (d) miRNA-429, (e) miRNA-199a, (f) single-base-mismatched miRNA-141, and (g) miRNA-141. (D) Stability evaluation of the PEC sensing platform with 10 fM miRNA-141 on PCE1 and 1 pM miRNA-141 on PCE2.
(A) Schematic layout and (B) photograph of ratiometric PEC paper analytical device; (C) Photograph of the assembled paper-based device.

Schematic illustration of (A) ratiometric PEC paper analytical device with dual-photocathode array for miRNA-141 detection, the color scheme of test pad is the same as in Scheme 1A. (B) Photogenerated charge transfer mechanism of cascaded multiple photosensitive structures (VB, valence band; CB, conduction band).

head.ce:title: Dual-photocathode array propelled lab-on-paper ratiometric photoelectrochemical sensing platform for ultrasensitive microRNA bioassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineerin g, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineerin g

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cInstitute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a dual-photocathode array propelled lab-on-paper ratiometric photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform integrated with DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy was engineered for ultrasensitive assay of microRNA-141 (miRNA-141). Specifically, the spatial-resolved dual-photocathode array were comprised of a working electrode (PCE1) and internal reference electrode (PCE2), which were responsible for producing independent working signal (Iw ) and internal reference signal (Ir ). The dual-photocathode array was assembled with cascading multiple photosensitive structures consisting of pyramid-shaped Cu2O, graphene quantum dots, and AgI nanoparticles (NPs). With the proximity regulation of DNA hairpins H1 and H2, the amplified photocurrent signals were obtained on PCE1 and PCE2. When target existed, the output DNA probes from various concentrations of miRNA-141 induced duplex specific nuclease-catalyzed target recycling reactions were introduced onto PCE1, while the output DNA probes produced with a constant concentration of miRNA-141 were introduced onto PCE2, resulting in the formation of different amounts of DNA bridge nanostructures on PCE1 and PCE2. Those formed DNA bridge nanostructures with enhanced rigidity remarkably increased the electron-transfer tunneling distance between the sensing interface and AgI NPs immobilized on the terminals of H1 and H2, leading to noticeably decreased photocurrent signals. Based on the introduction of different amounts of target-induced output DNA probes on PCE1 and PCE2, the variable Iw and constant Ir were ultimately collected. By monitoring the ratio of Iw and Ir , the ultrasensitive bioassay of miRNA-141 was realized with high accuracy, selectivity, and practicability.

ce:section.ce:para: By virtue of the integrated merits of cellulose paper and photoelectrochemical (PEC) technique, such as portability, low cost, easy operation, and high sensitivity, microfluidic paper-based PEC analytical devices have attracted considerable interest in recent years [1–4]. Despite that these advantages have motivated great successes in the paper-based PEC analytical fields, the sensitivity and selectivity for the practical PEC bioanalysis applications are still unsatisfactory. The imperfections mainly stem from the signal readout mode and selected photoelectrode type. For the signal readout mode, a majority of reported paper-based PEC biosensors adopt a single-signal readout mode (i.e., “signal-on” or “signal-off”) [5,6]. Such a single-signal assay strategy would bring about false positive or negative errors because the photocurrent intensity is easily subjected to the disturbances caused by environmental or instrumental factors [7,8]. To solve this issue, dual-signal ratiometric assay strategy emerges at the right moment, which can offer a self-calibration to minimize the internal/external interference, and thus possess improved accuracy and sensitivity [9,10]. Although the ratiometric PEC detection techniques based on different critical voltages or excitation wavelengths of photosensitive species have been proposed in previous work [11,12], there is relatively little progress in the development of ratiometric PEC biosensor. This is primarily because of the hardship in gaining a pair of photosensitive materials with distinguishable excitation potentials or wavelengths, which can generate independent photocurrents in a detection run. Consequently, defeating the severe dependence of ratiometric PEC analysis on photosensitive species to exploit a more universal lab-on-paper ratiometric PEC sensing platform is highly desirable.

For the selected photoelectrode type, most of the currently developed PEC paper analytical devices belong to photoanode-based signal type and the photocurrent signal is generated by the hole-oxidation reaction at photoanode/electrolyte interface [13,14], which thus unavoidably suffers from the interference from coexisting reductive substances and results in a poor selectivity [15]. Fortunately, photocathode-based PEC assay can conquer the intrinsic defect of photoanode-based type due to the fact that the electron-reduction reaction occurred on the photocathode/electrolyte interface, which can well escape from the disturbance of coexisting reductive molecules [16]. Regrettably, the paper-based photocathodic sensing platform has been rarely exploited. Thus, it is highly demanded to develop a lab-on-paper cathode PEC analytical device for practical bioanalysis application.

Herein, a dual-photocathode array propelled lab-on-paper ratiometric PEC sensing platform integrated with DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy was proposed for ultrasensitive determination of microRNA-141 (miRNA-141) as model analyte. As displayed in Scheme 2 A, the pyramid-shaped Cu2O was first assembled on Au NPs functionalized dual photocathodes (PCE1 and PCE2) and further sensitized by GQDs and AgI NPs with the assistance of DNA hairpins H1 and H2, obtaining the cascaded multiple photosensitive structures with the generation of amplified photocurrent signals. In the presence of target, the DSN-assisted target recycling amplification was performed to translate a small quantity of target miRNA-141 into plentiful output DNA probes. Then, the output DNA probes from various concentrations of miRNA-141 induced DSN-catalyzed target recycling reactions were introduced onto PCE1, while the output DNA probes produced with a constant concentration of miRNA-141 were introduced onto PCE2, resulting in the formation of different amounts of DNA bridge nanostructures on PCE1 and PCE2 with the aid of incubated bridging strand (BS). Those formed DNA bridge nanostructures with enhanced rigidity compelled the AgI NPs immobilized on the terminals of H1 and H2 away from sensing interface spatially and resulted in the remarkably decreased photocurrent signals. Based on the introduction of different amounts of target-induced output DNA probes on PCE1 and PCE2, the variable Iw and constant Ir were ultimately recorded. By monitoring the ratio of Iw from PCE1 and Ir from PCE2, the ultrasensitive assay of miRNA-141 with varied levels was achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and operation of ratiometric PEC paper analytical device
ce:section.ce:para: Enlightened by the praiseworthy merits of the origami-based device, a ratiometric PEC paper analytical device was ingeniously designed (details in the Supplementary Material). Scheme 1A,B described the device layout consisting of a test pad and auxiliary pad. Typically, the dual-photocathode array was constructed on the test pad, and an injection hole was placed between the electrodes array. To enhance the conductivity and surface area of paper chip, the all-around Au NPs functionalization was first conducted on the electrodes array (Fig. S1A,B). Then, the rectangular hydrophobic conductive regions were introduced onto the middle of electrodes array by secondary wax-printing (Scheme 1B and Fig. S1C,D), which were employed to ensure the discrete spot assay based on its electric-electric structure. The elliptical unprinted region on the auxiliary pad was applied to the screen-printing of carbon counter electrode and Ag/AgCl reference electrode (Scheme 1A,B and Fig. S1). And the two rectangular unprinted regions were used as incisions for the exposure of dual-photocathode array after they were cut from the auxiliary pad (Scheme 1B).

The aforesaid injection hole and incisions were cut by using a 2-mm-diameter perforator and a scissor, respectively. After cutting, the resultant test pad and auxiliary pad with the same size were first folded into the final configuration and then it was clamped with the help of binder clips (Scheme 1B,C). Finally, the assembled paper-based device was activated by the addition of detection solution into paper assay cell through the injection hole, which was connected to the analytical instrument via the alligator clips.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of pyramid-shaped Cu2O on dual-photocathode array
ce:section.ce:para: The pyramid-shaped Cu2O was deposited on the Au NPs functionalized PCE1 and PCE2 (Au-PCE1 and Au-PCE2) from a basic solution containing copper acetate (0.02 M) and lactate (0.4 M). The pH of plating solution was adjusted by adding a controlled amount of NaOH solution. During the electrodeposition processes, the deposition temperature was maintained at 60 °C via a water bath. The deposition time was 40 min and the applied potential was -0.2 V. After the electrodeposition procedures, the Cu2O/Au-PCE1 and Cu2O/Au-PCE2 were obtained.

The DSN-catalyzed target recycling reaction was carried out by mixing different concentrations of targets with annealed hairpin probe (HP) solution, 0.5 U DSN enzyme, and 1 × DSN master buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM dithiothreitol, 5 mM MgCl2, pH 8.0). After the mixture was incubated at 60 °C for 1 h, the DSN enzyme was denatured via adding the DSN stop solution (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid), followed by reacting at 50 °C for 10 min. To absolutely inactivate the activity of DSN enzyme, the resultant mixture was heated at 90 °C for 20 min and then cooled to room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric PEC sensing array
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the immobilization, the dual photocathodes was first functionalized with amino groups by reacting with 3-aminopropyltriet hoxysilane (APTES). Briefly, 20 μL of APTES (2 wt%) was dropped onto the surface of Cu2O/Au-PCE1 and Cu2O/Au-PCE2 and incubated for 40 min. After washing, the electrodes array was immersed in 20 μL of GQDs solution containing 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (20 mM) and N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide (20 mM) for 50 min. During this process, the GQDs (the detailed preparation processes see the Supplementary Material) were grafted on the electrodes array surface via amidation [13]. Next, 20 μL of the mixture containing AgI NPs labeled DNA hairpins H1 and H2 (H1-AgI and H2-AgI) (the detailed preparation processes in the Supplementary Material) was incubated on GQDs-modified electrodes array for 2 h, followed by washing. Thereafter, the obtained dual photocathodes were blocked with 20 μL of 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) solution (1 mM) for 1 h to eliminate the nonspecific binding sites. For miRNA-141 detection, 20 μL of the products from various concentrations of miRNA-141 (0.2 fM to 1 nM) induced DSN-catalyzed target recycling reactions were dropped onto the modified Cu2O/Au-PCE1, while the products obtained with a constant concentration of miRNA-141 (1 pM) were added into the modified Cu2O/Au-PCE2. After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, the resultant electrodes array was rinsed with washing buffer and further incubated with 20 μL of 5.0 μM BS for 1 h. Finally, the obtained dual-photocathode array was washed and used for the detections of Iw and Ir .

The dual-signal measurement was performed by injecting 120 μL phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (0.1 M, pH 7.4) into a paper assay cell. The xenon lamp was maintained on, and the applied potential was set at 0.0 V. The connection between PCE1 and analytical instrument was first established to record Iw . After 10 s, the connection was cut off, and there was no signal output. For the next 10 s, the Ir was collected via the connection between PCE2 and analytical instrument. The continuous dual PEC signals output was acquired by repeating above operation processes.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images revealed that the pyramid-shaped Cu2O were compactly grown on the surface of Au NPs functionalized tangled cellulose fibers network (Fig. 1 A, B and D, E). The densely well-developed Cu2O pyramid (Fig. 1C) could provide plenty of binding sites for the subsequent loading of nanomaterials and biomolecules. The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum confirmed the presence of Au, Cu, C, and O elements, which was consistent with the EDS element mapping images (Fig. 1F). Those results verified the successful fabrication of Cu2O/Au-PCE (PCE1 and PCE2 were abbreviated as PCE). The element chemical states and compositions of the as-prepared Cu2O/Au-PCE were further studied by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the XPS survey spectrum revealed the existence of Au 4f, C 1s, O 1s, and Cu 2p peaks. The high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2B) displayed a pair of peaks positioned at 952.3 eV and 932.4 eV, which were referred to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 binding energy of Cu+ in Cu2O [24]. The appearance of relatively weak satellite peaks assigned to Cu2+ species could be attributed to the slight surface oxidization of Cu2O in humid air [25]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) presented a prominent peak centered at 530.7, which was ascribed to the oxygen in Cu2O [18]. Besides, the two strong peaks at 83.4 eV (Au 4f7/2) and 87.2 eV (Au 4f5/2) in Au 4f spectrum (Fig. 2D) were the characteristics of Au NPs coated on the paper fibers [26]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis results gave an insight into the crystalline structure of Cu2O. As shown in Fig. 2E, the XRD patterns of Cu2O/Au-PCE (curve c) showed five additional peaks appeared at 29.4° (110), 36.4° (111), 42.3° (200), 61.2° (220), and 73.4° (311) except for the paper and Au peaks (curves a and b) [13]. Those emerging diffraction peaks were assigned to cubic Cu2O phase (JCPDS 05-0667) [27]. The strongest peak located at 36.4° manifested the (111) preferred orientation of Cu2O [18,28]. Besides, the Raman scattering was used to investigate the microstructure details of Cu2O and the Raman spectrum of Cu2O/Au-PCE was described in Fig. 2F. The weakest peak at 148 cm−1 was ascribed to the Raman scattering from phonons of symmetry [29], whereas the strongest peak at 218 cm−1 could be assigned to the second-order Raman active mode of Cu2O [27]. The three moderate peaks at 416, 515, and 626 cm−1 originated from the four phonon mode, Raman-allowed mode, and infrared-allowed mode, respectively [27,30]. Furthermore, the sharply defined mode of the most intense peak at 218 cm−1 confirmed that the as-prepared Cu2O possessed high structural quality [30].

Fig. 3 A displayed the TEM image of the synthesized monodisperse AgI NPs, which exhibited a near-spherical structure. The average diameter of the AgI NPs was about 4 nm (Fig. 3B). The EDS spectrum (Fig. 3C) revealed that there were Ag and I elements in the as-prepared nanoparticles. (Note: the observed Al element peak was from aluminum foil substrate). The approximative atomic ratio of Ag and I was 1:1, suggesting the triumphant synthesis of AgI NPs. XPS was employed to analyze the compositions and element chemical states of AgI NPs. The high-resolution Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 3D) clearly showed the Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 peaks at 373.8 eV and 367.7 eV, which were ascribed to Ag+ of AgI. In addition, the two peaks at 631.1 eV and 620.1 eV (Fig. 3E) were attributed to I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2 binding energies of I−. Those data were in accordance with previous research on AgI nanocrystals [31]. The crystal structures of AgI NPs were analyzed by XRD technique. As shown in Fig. 3F, the diffraction peaks at 22.4° (100), 23.8° (002), 25.3° (101), 32.8° (102), 39.2° (110), 42.6° (103), 45.6° (201), 46.3° (112), 51.9° (202), 59.3° (203), 63.2° (211), 66.7° (212), 71.1° (300), 73.5° (213), and 76.2° (302) were ascribed to hexagonal β-AgI (JCPDS 09-0374) [32]. In particular, the diffraction peaks at 23.8°, 39.2°, 46.3°, 63.2°, 71.1°, and 76.2° could be indexed to (111), (220), (311), (331), (422), and (511) crystal planes of cubic γ-AgI (JCPDS 09-0399) [33]. Owing to the fairly close reflection positions, it was difficult to assign the set of diffraction peaks to γ-AgI or β-AgI, accurately. However, the appearance of the diffraction peak at 56.8° (400) proved the formation of γ-AgI [20]. Those analysis results suggested that the synthesized sample was comprised of a mixture of cubic γ-AgI and hexagonal β-AgI phases. Besides, no impurities were observed in the XRD pattern, indicating the high purity of AgI NPs.

A schematic illustration of dual-photocathode array propelled ratiometric PEC paper analytical device for miRNA-141 detection was presented in Scheme 2A. In the absence of miRNA-141, both the DNA hairpins H1 and H2 adopted a stem-loop configuration [34] and maintained the labeled AgI NPs in close proximity to electrodes array surface, which allowed the unimpeded electron flow between AgI NPs and GQDs-modified PCE1 and PCE2, activating the co-sensitization effect. The electron-transfer mechanism of the formed cascaded multiple photosensitive structures was described in Scheme 2B. The well-matched band-edge levels of AgI NPs, GQDs, and Cu2O could effectively manipulate the directional charge migration and facilitate the charge separation [21,27,33], resulting in the generation of amplified photocurrent signals on PCE1 and PCE2. In the existence of miRNA-141, the DNA-RNA heteroduplexes were first formed based on the hybridization between HP and miRNA-141 [35], which were then subjected to DSN cleavage reaction accompanied by the output of DNA probe and the release of unbroken miRNA-141. The released miRNA-141 was recycled to hybridize with remainder HP and initiated more cycles of cleavage, release, and hybridization, generating massive output DNA probes. Afterward, the output DNA probes from different concentrations of miRNA-141 induced DSN-catalyzed target recycling reactions were introduced onto PCE1, while the output DNA probes produced with a constant concentration of miRNA-141 were introduced onto PCE2, which opened the hairpin configurations of surface-confined H1 and H2 by interacting with the loop strands of hairpins [36] and resulted in the formation of double-stranded DNA nanostructures acting as bridge pier. It was worth noting that the nucleotide sequences of DNA hairpins H1 and H2 were skillfully engineered to assure that they could all partially hybridize with output DNA probes. Finally, the bridge abutment was formed by the hybridization of the “3′ tail” and “5′ tail” sequences of BS with the remaining sequences of H1 and H2, respectively. The formed stable DNA bridge nanostructures with enhanced rigidity forced the labeled AgI NPs away from the sensing interface spatially and interdicted the electron transfer process, leading to the visibly decreased photocurrent signals. Based on the introduction of different amounts of target-induced output DNA probes on PCE1 and PCE2, the variable Iw and constant Ir were ultimately obtained. By calculating the ratio of Iw from PCE1 and Ir from PCE2, the ultrasensitive detection of miRNA-141 was realized.

As an effective technique for monitoring the interface properties of electrodes, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was employed to investigate the stepwise assembly processes of biosensor. Fig. 4 A displayed the EIS of PCE1 under different modification conditions. For the Au-PCE1, a lowest electron transfer resistance (R et) (curve a) was observed on account of the superior conductivity of Au NPs. After the electrodeposition of Cu2O, the R et increased (curve b), confirming that the Cu2O was resoundingly assembled on Au-PCE1 surface. When the resulting electrode was subjected to the sensitization of GQDs, the R et further increased (curve c), which could be explained that the negatively charged carboxyl groups of GQDs repelled the access of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− to electrode surface [2]. As expected, after the incubation of H1-AgI and H2-AgI on the modified electrode surface, a vastly increased R et was observed (curve d) due to the electrostatic repulsion between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and electronegative DNA [4] as well as the low conductivity of AgI NPs. Upon the further blocking by MCH, the R et continued to increase (curve e) owing to its poor charge-transfer capability. In the presence of target, the rigid DNA bridge nanostructures were assembled on the sensing interface with the aid of DSN-assisted target recycling reaction. A very much larger R et was observed (curve f) on account of the formed negatively charged interface. Therefore, the step by step increase in R et attested the successful construction of the PEC biosensor.

The construction process of the PEC biosensor could also be confirmed by the PEC behaviors of modified electrodes at different stages, as shown in Fig. 4B. For the Au-PCE deposited with Cu2O, an appropriate photocurrent response was observed (curve a). After the sensitization of GQDs, the photocurrent intensity overtly increased (curve b) owing to the formation of Cu2O/GQDs sensitization structure. Exhilaratingly, upon the further immobilization of H1-AgI and H2-AgI onto the modified electrodes, a largest photocurrent response was obtained (curve c) because of the completely activated co-sensitization effect. When the resultant sensing interface was blocked with MCH, the photocurrent intensity decreased (curve d) due to the poor charge-transfer capability of MCH. In the presence of target, the photocurrent response further decreased evidently (curve e), which was mainly attributed to that the target-dependent DNA bridge nanostructures with enhanced rigidity significantly increased the electron-transfer tunneling distance between the sensing interface and labeled AgI NPs, and further interdicted the electron-transfer process. It was remarkable that the diminution of photocurrent response of PCE1 treated with 100 pM miRNA-141 was conspicuous in contrast to that of PCE2 treated with 1 pM miRNA-141, which could not only prove the great signal amplification capability of the designed DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy but also the excellent work independence between PCE1 and PCE2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of paper-based ratiometric PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the paper-based ratiometric PEC sensing platform was evaluated under optimized experimental conditions (details in the Supplementary Material). With the increment of target concentrations, more DNA bridge nanostructures were assembled on PCE1 and the Iw from PCE1 stepwise decreased, whereas the Ir from PCE2 treated with constant concentration of target maintained unchanged (Fig. 5 A), which ensured the dual-signal ratiometric detection. As described in Fig. 5B, the ratio of Iw and Ir (Iw /Ir ) decreased in a linear manner as the logarithm of miRNA-141 concentrations increased from 0.2 fM to 1 nM. The linear regression equation was Iw /Ir = -1.0730lgc – 0.1711 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9961. The detection limit was estimated to be 67 aM (S/N = 3), which was comparable or even superior to that of previous work (Table S2). The high sensitivity was primarily attributed to the photocathode-based dual-signal ratiometric readout, DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy, and low background noise innate in PEC analysis.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of lab-on-paper ratiometric PEC biosensor, various interferences such as miRNA-21, miRNA-155, miRNA-429, miRNA-199a, and single-base-mismatched miRNA-141 were selected for interference test. As displayed in Fig. 5C, apart from the target miRNA-141, none of these interferences brought about apparent change of Iw /Ir compared with the blank test, despite that the concentrations of non-target miRNAs were 100 times higher than that of target miRNA-141. The comparison suggested that only the target miRNA-141 with well-matched sequence could hybridize with HP and induce the formation of DNA bridge nanostructures, achieving dual-signal ratiometric detection. Thus, the proposed PEC biosensor exhibited excellent selectivity. The stability of the photocurrent response of PEC biosensor was also assessed. As depicted in Fig. 5D, there were almost no changes in the photocurrent values obtained by 10 repetitive on/off irradiation cycles. Moreover, after a storage period of 2 weeks at 4 °C, no evident changes in the photocurrent response were observed. Those results indicated that the well-constructed PEC biosensor possessed good stability. In addition, the reproducibility of the ratiometric PEC bioassay was studied on the basis of the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for interassay and intraassay. The RSDs of interassay (five independent PEC sensing platforms treated with 1 pM miRNA-141 were prepared and detected under the same conditions) was 4.7 %, whereas the value for intraassay (the same PEC sensing platform toward 1 pM miRNA-141 was determined for five times) was 5.1 %, proving the acceptable reproducibility of the developed PEC biosensor for miRNA-141 detection.

The applicability of the proposed ratiometric PEC biosensor for miRNA-141 detection in real samples was further examined. The cell lysates were first extracted from human breast cancer cells with the high expression of miRNA-141 and then tested by the designed ratiometric PEC strategy. The experimental results were compared with the reference values provided by commercial fluorescence quantitative realtime polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) method. As shown in Table S3, the results of the two methods were in good accordance, which confirmed the great potentiality of the proposed lab-on-paper ratiometric PEC biosensor for clinical miRNAs diagnostics.

To summarize, a dual-photocathode array propelled paper-based ratiometric PEC sensing platform with DNA bridge nanostructure mediated electron-transfer tunneling distance regulation strategy was successfully constructed for ultrasensitive bioanalysis. The proposed work not only inherited the enhanced selectivity, high sensitivity, and persuasive accuracy of photocathode-based ratiometric assay but also exhibited good universality by overcoming the severe dependency of PEC ratiometry on photosensitive species. It could be flexibly expanded to detect other biomarkers, including DNA, proteins, and cells, etc. As a result, using miRNA-141 as a model analyte, the programmed paper-based ratiometric PEC biosensor realized the highly sensitive and selective detection of miRNA-141 in complicated real samples. The triumph in the construction of dual-photocathode array propelled ratiometric PEC paper analytical device opened up a new horizon to develop more lab-on-paper ratiometric PEC analytical systems for environment testing, food monitoring, biomedical research, and clinical diagnosis.

Hongmei Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Meiling Xu: Visualization, Formal analysis. Zhenglin Li: Investigation, Data curation. Shenguang Ge: Software, Visualization. Lina Zhang: Writing - review & editing, Resources. Peihua Zhu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jinghua Yu: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Taishan Scholars Program, Case-by-Case Project for Top Outstanding Talents of Jinan, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874055, 51872121), the project of "20 items of University" of Jinan (2018GXRC001), and Excellent Youth Innovation Team in Universities of Shandong (2019KJC016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128093.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible electronics based on micro/nanostructured paper

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, L. Zhang, K. Cui, S. Ge, X. Cheng, M. Yan, J. Yu, H. Liu, Flexible electronics based on micro/nanostructured paper, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1801588.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sudoku-like lab-on-paper cyto-device with dual enhancement of electrochemiluminescence intermediates strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yang, Y. Zhang, L. Li, L. Zhang, F. Lan, J. Yu, Sudoku-like lab-on-paper cyto-device with dual enhancement of electrochemiluminescence intermediates strategy, Anal. Chem. 89 (2017) 7511-7519.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance modified cellulose paper-based biosensors for medical diagnostics and early cancer screening: a concise review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ratajczak, M. Stobiecka, High-performance modified cellulose paper-based biosensors for medical diagnostics and early cancer screening: A concise review, Carbohyd. Polym. 229 (2020) 115463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal-switchable lab-on-paper photoelectrochemical aptasensing system integrated triple-helix molecular switch with charge separation and recombination regime of type-II CdTe@CdSe core-shell quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Hu, H. Yang, Z. Li, L. Zhang, P. Zhu, M. Yan, J. Yu, Signal-switchable lab-on-paper photoelectrochemical aptasensing system integrated triple-helix molecular switch with charge separation and recombination regime of type-II CdTe@CdSe core-shell quantum dots, Biosens. Bioelectron. 147 (2020) 111786.

Yu, Mimic peroxidase-transfer enhancement of photoelectrochemical aptasensing via CuO nanoflowers functionalized lab-on-paper device with a controllable fluid separator, Biosens. Bioelectron. 133 (2019) 32-38.

Yu, Addressable TiO2 nanotubes functionalized paper-based cyto-sensor with photocontrollable switch for highly-efficient evaluating surface protein expressions of cancer cells, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 13882-13890.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xu, Q. Liu, M. Xie, S. Huang, M. He, L. Huang, H. Xu, H. Li, Synthesis of zinc ferrite/silver iodide composite with enhanced photocatalytic antibacterial and pollutant degradation ability, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 528 (2018) 70-81.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Murugesan, S. Narayanan, M. Matheswaran, Photocatalytic performance and antibacterial activity of visible light driven silver iodide anchored on graphitic-C3N4 binary composite, Environ. Nanotechnol. Monit. Manag. 10 (2018) 253-263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DNA strand replacement mechanism in molecular beacons encoded for the detection of cancer biomarkers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Stobiecka, A. Chalupa, DNA strand replacement mechanism in molecular beacons encoded for the detection of cancer biomarkers, J. Phys. Chem. B, 120 (2016) 4782-4790.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical biosensing system for the detection of survivin mRNA in colorectal cancer cells using a graphene oxide carrier-bound oligonucleotide molecular beacon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ratajczak, E.B. Krazinski, E.A. Kowalczyk, B. Dworakowska, S. Jakiela, M. Stobiecka, Optical biosensing system for the detection of survivin mRNA in colorectal cancer cells using a graphene oxide carrier-bound oligonucleotide molecular beacon, Nanomaterials, 8 (2018) 510.
M. Stobiecka, A. Chałupa, Biosensors based on molecular beacons, Chem. Pap. 69 (2015) 62-76.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongmei Yang studies in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as PhD student.
Meiling Xu studies in the Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan as PhD student.
Zhenglin Li studies in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Shenguang Ge obtained his Ph.D. degree in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University in 2013. He is currently a professor at University of Jinan and he is working on the design and synthesis of functional materials, as well as their applications for biosensors and bioanalysis.

Lina Zhang obtained her PhD degree in Civil Engineering from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology in 2014. She is currently an assistant professor in the Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan. Her current scientific interests focus on nanomaterials, analytical chemistry and material engineering.

Peihua Zhu received her PhD in Inorganic Chemistry from Shandong University, in 2005. She is currently an associate professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. Her research interests include bio-hybrid materials for wearable energy devices and sensing devices using soft materials.

Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2004 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. She is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. Her research interests include lab-on-paper devices, functional nanomaterials, and portable electronic devices.
